1:
"The elder unto the elect lady and her children, whom I love in the truth; and not I only, but also all they that have known the truth;" The witnesses note the address to the elect lady — the crux of her identity. On "the elder," the grammatical voices note the writer. Barnes and Robertson note the self-designation: "the elder (presbyteros)" — John writes simply as "the elder," a title of age and apostolic authority; the aged apostle, well known, needs no further name.

On "unto the elect lady and her children," interpreters divide over the recipient. The grammatical-historical Barnes, Ellicott, and Robertson, with Clarke, hold that "the elect (chosen) lady (kyria)" is a literal Christian woman — addressed as an individual, not a church, since the letter speaks of "her children" and is personal in tone; some take "Kyria" (or "Electa") as her proper name, others as an honorable, unnamed lady. The Reformed Gill and others hold that "the elect lady" is figurative — a particular church (or the church universal) addressed under the delicate image of a chosen lady, "her children" being its members. Robertson notes the language can be read either way.

On "whom I love in the truth; and not I only, but also all they that have known the truth," the witnesses note the bond of truth. Barnes and Wesley note that John loves the lady (and her children) "in the truth (sincerely, and in the bond of the gospel truth)," and that "all they that have known the truth" share this love; the truth is the bond uniting all believers in love. The verse displays the address to the elect lady, loved in the truth.

The witnesses agree the substance: the aged apostle writes with love, in the bond of truth shared by all believers, to the elect lady and her children (Barnes, Robertson, Gill, Wesley). But the identity of "the elect lady" divides them: a literal Christian woman, named or unnamed (Barnes, Ellicott, Clarke), or a particular church figuratively addressed (Gill, the figurative view). The verse displays the elect-lady crux. The verse marks the writer: the elder. The verse displays the recipient: the elect lady. The verse displays the debate: a literal woman or a church. The verse displays the bond: love in the truth. The address displays the apostle writing in love to the elect lady. The verse displays the elder: John. The verse displays the lady: elect. The verse displays the truth: the bond. The address to the elect lady, loved in the truth, displays the opening and a famous crux: the elder writes to the elect lady and her children, loved by all who know the truth — read as a literal Christian woman (Barnes, Ellicott, Clarke) or as a church figuratively addressed (Gill). The verse displays the loved lady, the bond being the indwelling truth (v.2).
2:
"For the truth's sake, which dwelleth in us, and shall be with us for ever." The brief verse notes the indwelling truth that abides forever. The grammatical voices note the ground of love. Barnes and Robertson note the reason for the love just expressed (v.1): "for the truth's sake, which dwelleth (abides) in us, and shall be with us for ever" — the love is grounded in the truth that dwells in believers; the gospel truth, abiding within, is the bond and reason of their mutual love.

On "which dwelleth in us," the witnesses note the indwelling. Barnes and JFB note that the truth "dwelleth in us" — it is not merely held intellectually but abides within as a living, indwelling reality; the truth received and lodged in the heart.

On "shall be with us for ever," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the permanence: the truth "shall be with us for ever" — the indwelling truth is not temporary but eternal; it abides forever in those who have received it; the permanence of the truth grounds the permanence of the love it produces. The verse displays the indwelling truth that abides forever.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the ground — "for the truth's sake, which dwelleth (abides) in us," the love grounded in the indwelling truth (Barnes, Robertson); "which dwelleth in us," the truth a living indwelling reality, lodged in the heart (Barnes, JFB); "shall be with us for ever," the truth eternal, abiding forever, grounding the permanence of love (Wesley). The verse displays the abiding truth. The verse marks the ground: the truth's sake. The verse displays the indwelling: in us. The verse displays the permanence: for ever. The verse displays the bond: truth and love. The truth displays the indwelling, eternal ground of the believers' love. The verse displays the truth: dwelling. The verse displays the abiding: forever. The verse displays the love: grounded. The indwelling truth that abides forever displays the ground of Christian love: the love is for the truth's sake — the truth that dwells in believers and shall be with them forever, the eternal indwelling reality that binds them in love. The verse displays the abiding truth, the source of grace, mercy, and peace (v.3).
3:
"Grace be with you, mercy, and peace, from God the Father, and from the Lord Jesus Christ, the Son of the Father, in truth and love." The witnesses note the apostolic blessing of grace, mercy, and peace from the Father and the Son. On "grace be with you, mercy, and peace," the grammatical voices note the threefold benediction. Barnes and Robertson note the greeting: "grace (God's favour) be with you, mercy (compassion to the needy and guilty), and peace (the resulting tranquility)" — the apostolic blessing of grace, mercy, and peace, the full sum of gospel blessing.

On "from God the Father, and from the Lord Jesus Christ, the Son of the Father," the witnesses note the divine source. Barnes and Wesley note that these blessings come "from God the Father, and from the Lord Jesus Christ, the Son of the Father" — the Father and the Son are joined as the source of the blessing; the coupling of Christ with the Father, and the title "the Son of the Father," affirm Christ's divine sonship.

On "in truth and love," the eastern Cyril notes the element: the blessing is bestowed and received "in truth and love" — truth and love are the sphere in which grace, mercy, and peace operate; the two great notes of the epistle (truth and love) frame the benediction; gospel blessing is enjoyed in the union of truth and love. The verse displays the apostolic blessing of grace, mercy, and peace from the Father and the Son.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the benediction — "grace... mercy, and peace," the threefold apostolic blessing, the sum of gospel blessing (Barnes, Robertson); "from God the Father, and from the Lord Jesus Christ, the Son of the Father," the Father and Son joined as source, affirming Christ's divine sonship (Barnes, Wesley); "in truth and love," the sphere of the blessing, the two notes of the epistle (Cyril). The verse displays the blessing. The verse marks the gifts: grace, mercy, peace. The verse displays the source: Father and Son. The verse displays the sonship: Son of the Father. The verse displays the sphere: truth and love. The blessing displays gospel grace from the Father and Son in truth and love. The verse displays the grace: bestowed. The verse displays the Son: of the Father. The verse displays the truth: and love. The apostolic blessing of grace, mercy, and peace from the Father and the Son displays the salutation: grace, mercy, and peace be with you from God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ, the Son of the Father, in truth and love. The verse displays the blessing in truth, leading to joy over children walking in truth (v.4).
4:
"I rejoiced greatly that I found of thy children walking in truth, as we have received a commandment from the Father." The witnesses note John's joy at finding her children walking in truth. On "I rejoiced greatly that I found of thy children walking in truth," the grammatical voices note the apostle's gladness. Barnes and Robertson note the cause of joy: "I rejoiced greatly that I found of thy children (some of them) walking in truth (living according to the gospel)" — John was greatly glad to find that some of the lady's children were walking in the truth; their faithful, godly walk gladdened the apostle's heart.

On "of thy children," the witnesses note the partial reference. Barnes and Ellicott note that "of thy children" (some of them) may simply mean those he had met, not implying that others were unfaithful; he rejoiced over those he found walking rightly.

On "as we have received a commandment from the Father," the puritan Henry notes the standard: their walking in truth was "as we have received a commandment from the Father" — in accordance with God's command; their conduct conformed to the Father's commandment; walking in truth is obedience to the divine command. The verse displays John's joy at finding her children walking in truth.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the gladness — "I rejoiced greatly that I found of thy children walking in truth," John glad to find the lady's children living according to the gospel (Barnes, Robertson); "of thy children" some of them, those he had met, not implying others unfaithful (Barnes, Ellicott); "as we have received a commandment from the Father," their walk conforming to God's command (Henry). The verse displays the joyful walk. The verse marks the joy: rejoiced greatly. The verse displays the finding: children in truth. The verse displays the walk: in truth. The verse displays the standard: the Father's commandment. The joy displays the apostle gladdened by faithful children. The verse displays the rejoicing: great. The verse displays the children: walking in truth. The verse displays the commandment: the Father's. John's joy at finding her children walking in truth displays the apostle's gladness: he rejoiced greatly to find the lady's children walking in the truth, according to the commandment received from the Father. The verse displays the children in truth, leading to the command to love one another (v.5).
5:
"And now I beseech thee, lady, not as though I wrote a new commandment unto thee, but that which we had from the beginning, that we love one another." The witnesses note the entreaty to love one another, the old commandment. On "I beseech thee, lady, not as though I wrote a new commandment," the grammatical voices note the appeal. Barnes and Robertson note John's tender entreaty: "I beseech (entreat, ask) thee, lady, not as though I wrote a new commandment unto thee" — John appeals to the lady, careful to say he is not laying down a new command; the entreaty is gentle, not authoritarian.

On "but that which we had from the beginning," the witnesses note the old command. Barnes and Wesley note that the command is "that which we had from the beginning (from the first preaching of the gospel)" — not a novelty but the original, foundational command of the gospel; the same love commanded from the start (cf. 1 John 2:7).

On "that we love one another," the puritan Henry notes the substance: the commandment is "that we love one another" — mutual love among believers is the command John presses on the lady; the great commandment, old yet ever fresh, is the heart of his appeal. The verse displays the entreaty to love one another, the old commandment.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the appeal — "I beseech thee, lady, not as though I wrote a new commandment," John's gentle entreaty, not a new command (Barnes, Robertson); "that which we had from the beginning," the original gospel command, not a novelty (1 John 2:7, Barnes, Wesley); "that we love one another," mutual love the substance, old yet fresh (Henry). The verse displays the command of love. The verse marks the entreaty: I beseech thee. The verse displays the command: not new. The verse displays the antiquity: from the beginning. The verse displays the substance: love one another. The appeal displays the old command of mutual love pressed. The verse displays the beseeching: gentle. The verse displays the command: old. The verse displays the love: enjoined. The entreaty to love one another, the old commandment, displays John's gentle appeal: he beseeches the lady, not with a new command but the one held from the beginning — that we love one another. The verse displays the command of love, defined as walking in his commandments (v.6).
6:
"And this is love, that we walk after his commandments. This is the commandment, That, as ye have heard from the beginning, ye should walk in it." The brief verse notes that love is walking after God's commandments. The grammatical voices note the definition of love. Barnes and Robertson note the equation: "this is love, that we walk after (according to) his commandments" — love is defined as obedience; to love is to walk in God's commandments; genuine love expresses itself in keeping God's commands (cf. John 14:15).

On "this is the commandment," the witnesses note the circle. Barnes and Wesley note the interweaving of love and command: "this is the commandment, That... ye should walk in it" — the commandment is that they walk in love; love and obedience are mutually defining; to walk in love is to keep the commandment, and the commandment is to walk in love.

On "as ye have heard from the beginning, ye should walk in it," the grammatical-historical note the continuity: they should walk in it "as ye have heard from the beginning" — in accordance with the original teaching; the command to walk in love is the same heard from the first; consistency from the beginning marks the true command. The verse displays that love is walking after God's commandments.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the definition — "this is love, that we walk after his commandments," love defined as obedience, keeping God's commands (John 14:15, Barnes, Robertson); "this is the commandment, That... ye should walk in it," love and obedience mutually defining (Barnes, Wesley); "as ye have heard from the beginning," the command consistent from the first (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the love that obeys. The verse marks the love: walking in commandments. The verse displays the commandment: walk in it. The verse displays the continuity: from the beginning. The verse displays the union: love and obedience. The definition displays love expressed as obedience to God's commands. The verse displays the love: obedient. The verse displays the commandment: kept. The verse displays the walk: in love. That love is walking after God's commandments displays the mutual definition of love and obedience: this is love, that we walk after his commandments — and the commandment, heard from the beginning, is that we walk in love. The verse displays the obedient love, set against the deceivers who deny Christ (v.7).
7:
"For many deceivers are entered into the world, who confess not that Jesus Christ is come in the flesh. This is a deceiver and an antichrist." The witnesses note the warning of deceivers who deny Christ come in the flesh. On "many deceivers are entered into the world," the grammatical voices note the danger. Barnes and Robertson note the warning: "many deceivers (those who lead astray) are entered (gone out) into the world" — the reason for the emphasis on truth and love is the presence of many deceivers in the world; the false teachers who endanger the church.

On "who confess not that Jesus Christ is come in the flesh," the witnesses note the docetic error. Barnes and Wesley note the mark of the deceivers: "who confess not (deny) that Jesus Christ is come in the flesh (has truly come as a real man, incarnate)" — the deceivers deny the genuine incarnation; the same docetic/gnostic error opposed in 1 John 4:2-3; the denial of Christ's true coming in the flesh.

On "this is a deceiver and an antichrist," the puritan Henry notes the identification: "this is a deceiver and an antichrist" — the one who denies the incarnation is both a deceiver (leading others astray) and an antichrist (opposed to Christ); the denial of Christ come in the flesh is the antichristian mark. The verse displays the warning of deceivers who deny Christ come in the flesh.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the danger — "many deceivers are entered into the world," the presence of many false teachers the reason for vigilance (Barnes, Robertson); "who confess not that Jesus Christ is come in the flesh," the docetic denial of the true incarnation (1 John 4:2-3, Barnes, Wesley); "this is a deceiver and an antichrist," the denier both deceiver and antichrist (Henry). The verse displays the deceivers. The verse marks the danger: many deceivers. The verse displays the error: deny Christ in the flesh. The verse displays the identification: deceiver and antichrist. The verse displays the mark: the antichristian denial. The warning displays the deceivers denying the incarnate Christ. The verse displays the deceivers: many. The verse displays the denial: of the flesh. The verse displays the antichrist: identified. The warning of deceivers who deny Christ come in the flesh displays the peril the lady must guard against: many deceivers have entered the world who deny that Jesus Christ has come in the flesh — and such a one is a deceiver and an antichrist. The verse displays the antichristian deceivers, against losing one's reward (v.8).
8:
"Look to yourselves, that we lose not those things which we have wrought, but that we receive a full reward." The brief verse notes the warning to guard against losing one's reward. The grammatical voices note the self-watch. Barnes and Robertson note the exhortation to vigilance: "look to yourselves (take heed, watch), that we lose not those things which we have wrought (the fruits of labour, what has been gained)" — in view of the deceivers (v.7), the readers are to watch lest they lose the spiritual gains they have made; vigilance against being robbed by error.

On "that we lose not those things which we have wrought," the witnesses note the danger of loss. Barnes and Wesley note that the false teachers threaten to undo the work of grace; the readers could lose what they had attained — the fruits of their faith and labour — if drawn away by the deceivers; the warning against forfeiting spiritual progress.

On "that we receive a full reward," the puritan note the goal: the aim is "that we receive a full (complete) reward" — not a diminished but a full reward; faithful perseverance, guarding against the deceivers, secures the complete reward; the loss of vigilance risks a diminished reward. The verse displays the warning to guard against losing one's reward.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the vigilance — "look to yourselves (take heed), that we lose not those things which we have wrought," watching lest they lose their spiritual gains to error (Barnes, Robertson); the deceivers threatening to undo the work of grace, the danger of forfeiting progress (Barnes, Wesley); "that we receive a full reward," faithful perseverance securing the complete reward (puritan). The verse displays the guarded reward. The verse marks the watch: look to yourselves. The verse displays the danger: lose what is wrought. The verse displays the goal: a full reward. The verse displays the threat: the deceivers. The warning displays vigilance securing the full reward. The verse displays the looking: to selves. The verse displays the loss: avoided. The verse displays the reward: full. The warning to guard against losing one's reward displays the call to vigilance amid the deceivers: take heed to yourselves, that you lose not what you have gained, but receive a full reward. The verse displays the guarded reward, grounded in abiding in Christ's doctrine (v.9).
9:
"Whosoever transgresseth, and abideth not in the doctrine of Christ, hath not God. He that abideth in the doctrine of Christ, he hath both the Father and the Son." The witnesses note that abiding in Christ's doctrine is to have the Father and the Son. On "whosoever transgresseth, and abideth not in the doctrine of Christ, hath not God," the grammatical voices note the test of doctrine. Barnes and Robertson note the warning: "whosoever transgresseth (goes beyond, oversteps), and abideth not in the doctrine of Christ (the teaching about Christ, or which Christ taught), hath not God" — the one who oversteps and does not remain in the true doctrine of Christ does not have God; departure from the doctrine of Christ is departure from God himself.

On "transgresseth... abideth not," the witnesses note the going beyond. Barnes and Wesley note that "transgresseth (proagon — runs ahead, goes too far)" describes the false teacher who, claiming advanced or progressive knowledge, oversteps the bounds of Christ's doctrine; such progress is apostasy; abiding, not advancing beyond, marks the faithful.

On "he that abideth in the doctrine of Christ, he hath both the Father and the Son," the puritan Henry notes the possession: "he that abideth (remains) in the doctrine of Christ, he hath both the Father and the Son" — the one who holds fast the true doctrine of Christ possesses both the Father and the Son; to have the true Christ (by abiding in his doctrine) is to have the Father also; sound doctrine and the possession of God go together. The verse displays that abiding in Christ's doctrine is to have the Father and the Son.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the test — "whosoever transgresseth, and abideth not in the doctrine of Christ, hath not God," departing from Christ's doctrine is departing from God (Barnes, Robertson); "transgresseth (runs ahead)," the false teacher overstepping under pretense of advanced knowledge, apostasy (Barnes, Wesley); "he that abideth in the doctrine of Christ, he hath both the Father and the Son," sound doctrine and the possession of God together (Henry). The verse displays the test of doctrine. The verse marks the transgressor: abides not. The verse displays the loss: hath not God. The verse displays the abider: in Christ's doctrine. The verse displays the possession: the Father and the Son. The test displays abiding in Christ's doctrine as having God. The verse displays the doctrine: of Christ. The verse displays the abiding: required. The verse displays the possession: Father and Son. That abiding in Christ's doctrine is to have the Father and the Son displays the doctrinal test: whoever oversteps and does not abide in the doctrine of Christ does not have God, but the one who abides in it has both the Father and the Son. The verse displays the doctrine of Christ, the boundary for hospitality (v.10).
10:
"If there come any unto you, and bring not this doctrine, receive him not into your house, neither bid him God speed:" The witnesses note the prohibition against receiving and greeting the false teacher. On "if there come any unto you, and bring not this doctrine," the grammatical voices note the case. Barnes and Robertson note the situation: "if there come any unto you, and bring not this doctrine (the true doctrine of Christ, v.9)" — the case is of a false teacher, one who does not bring the true doctrine of Christ, coming to the lady or the church; the traveling teacher who denies the incarnation.

On "receive him not into your house," the witnesses note the refusal of hospitality. Barnes and Wesley note the command: "receive him not into your house (do not lodge or harbour him)" — the false teacher is not to be received into the home; this concerns not ordinary courtesy to all men, but the refusal to host and support one spreading damnable error; to house him is to aid his work.

On "neither bid him God speed," the puritan Henry notes the withholding of greeting: "neither bid him God speed (do not greet him, do not wish him success)" — not even a greeting of approval or wish for his success is to be given; the warm greeting would signal endorsement; the believer must not, by hospitality or greeting, lend countenance to the deceiver. (Interpreters note this is the refusal of fellowship and support, not a license for incivility to all.) The verse displays the prohibition against receiving and greeting the false teacher.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the case — "if there come any unto you, and bring not this doctrine," a false teacher denying the true doctrine of Christ coming to them (Barnes, Robertson); "receive him not into your house," refusing to host and support the spreader of error, lest one aid his work (Barnes, Wesley); "neither bid him God speed," withholding the greeting that would signal endorsement, not a license for incivility (Henry). The verse displays the refusal. The verse marks the teacher: brings not the doctrine. The verse displays the refusal: not into your house. The verse displays the withholding: no greeting. The verse displays the reason: no endorsement. The prohibition displays the false teacher denied hospitality and greeting. The verse displays the false teacher: refused. The verse displays the house: closed. The verse displays the greeting: withheld. The prohibition against receiving and greeting the false teacher displays the protective command: if anyone comes not bringing the true doctrine of Christ, do not receive him into your house nor bid him God speed — lest you endorse and aid his error. The verse displays the closed door, lest one share the deceiver's evil (v.11).
11:
"For he that biddeth him God speed is partaker of his evil deeds." The brief verse notes that greeting the false teacher makes one a partaker of his evil. The grammatical voices note the reason for the prohibition. Barnes and Robertson note the ground of v.10: "he that biddeth him God speed (greets him, wishes him success) is partaker (koinonei — shares, has fellowship) of his evil deeds" — to greet and endorse the false teacher is to share in his evil works; the one who countenances the deceiver becomes a participant in the harm he does.

On "is partaker of his evil deeds," the witnesses note the shared guilt. Barnes and Wesley note that by encouraging or aiding the false teacher, one becomes complicit in his error and its consequences; the endorsement makes one a partner in the evil; neutrality toward soul-destroying error is not innocent.

On the principle, the puritan note the seriousness: to lend support or even approval to those who spread damnable error is to share their guilt; the believer's duty is to refuse all countenance to the enemies of Christ's truth, lest he partake of their sin. The verse displays that greeting the false teacher makes one a partaker of his evil.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the reason — "he that biddeth him God speed is partaker (shares fellowship) of his evil deeds," greeting and endorsing the false teacher sharing in his evil works (Barnes, Robertson); encouraging or aiding the deceiver making one complicit, neutrality toward error not innocent (Barnes, Wesley); lending approval to error sharing the guilt (puritan). The verse displays the shared guilt. The verse marks the greeting: God speed. The verse displays the participation: partaker of evil. The verse displays the complicity: shared guilt. The verse displays the duty: refuse countenance. The reason displays the greeter complicit in the deceiver's evil. The verse displays the greeting: condemned. The verse displays the partaking: of evil. The verse displays the guilt: shared. That greeting the false teacher makes one a partaker of his evil displays the reason for the closed door: the one who bids him God speed shares in his evil deeds — to countenance the deceiver is to become a partner in his sin. The verse displays the shared guilt, closing the warning with the apostle's hope to visit (v.12).
12:
"Having many things to write unto you, I would not write with paper and ink: but I trust to come unto you, and speak face to face, that our joy may be full." The witnesses note John's wish to speak face to face that joy may be full. On "having many things to write unto you, I would not write with paper and ink," the grammatical voices note the preference for personal visit. Barnes and Robertson note the apostle's choice: "having many things to write unto you, I would not (did not wish to) write with paper (papyrus) and ink" — John has much more to say but prefers not to put it all in a letter; the written word is not his chosen means for the fuller communication.

On "I trust to come unto you, and speak face to face," the witnesses note the hoped-for visit. Barnes and Ellicott note John's hope: "I trust (hope) to come unto you, and speak face to face (mouth to mouth, in person)" — he hopes to visit and speak personally; the personal presence is far better than the letter for full communication.

On "that our joy may be full," the puritan Henry notes the aim: the purpose of the visit is "that our joy may be full (complete)" — the mutual joy of personal fellowship completes what writing cannot; face-to-face communion brings a fullness of joy the letter cannot give; the goal of the visit is the perfecting of joy. The verse displays John's wish to speak face to face that joy may be full.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the preference — "having many things to write... I would not write with paper and ink," John preferring not to put the fuller communication in a letter (Barnes, Robertson); "I trust to come unto you, and speak face to face," hoping to visit and speak in person, better than the letter (Barnes, Ellicott); "that our joy may be full," personal fellowship completing the joy (Henry). The verse displays the hoped-for visit. The verse marks the writing: not paper and ink. The verse displays the hope: to come. The verse displays the means: face to face. The verse displays the aim: joy made full. The wish displays the apostle longing for personal fellowship. The verse displays the writing: deferred. The verse displays the visit: hoped. The verse displays the joy: full. John's wish to speak face to face that joy may be full displays the warm close of the letter: having much to say, he prefers not to write it all but hopes to come and speak in person, that their mutual joy may be complete. The verse displays the hoped-for fellowship, with greetings from the sister's children (v.13).
13:
"The children of thy elect sister greet thee. Amen." The brief verse notes the closing greeting from the elect sister's children. The grammatical voices note the salutation. Barnes and Robertson note the final greeting: "the children of thy elect (chosen) sister greet (salute) thee" — the letter closes with greetings from the children of the lady's "elect sister"; whether a literal sister (whose children send greetings) or, on the figurative reading, the members of a sister church.

On "thy elect sister," the witnesses note the parallel to v.1. Barnes and Ellicott note that "elect sister" parallels "elect lady" (v.1); on the literal reading, the lady's sister, also a Christian; on the figurative reading, a sister congregation; the same interpretive question as the elect lady recurs here.

On "greet thee. Amen," the grammatical-historical note the warm close: the greeting conveys the affection of the wider Christian family; "Amen" (in some texts) closes the brief letter; the bond of love among believers, expressed in mutual greeting, fittingly ends the epistle on truth and love. The verse displays the closing greeting from the elect sister's children.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the salutation — "the children of thy elect sister greet thee," the letter closing with greetings from the elect sister's children (Barnes, Robertson); "elect sister" paralleling "elect lady" (v.1), literally a sister or figuratively a sister church (Barnes, Ellicott); the greeting conveying the affection of the Christian family, closing the letter on love (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the closing greeting. The verse marks the greeting: from the sister's children. The verse displays the parallel: elect sister. The verse displays the bond: Christian family. The verse displays the close: Amen. The salutation displays the wider family greeting the lady in love. The verse displays the children: greeting. The verse displays the sister: elect. The verse displays the close: warm. The closing greeting from the elect sister's children displays the warm end of the brief letter: the children of the lady's elect sister send their greetings — the bond of love among believers, expressed in mutual greeting, fittingly closing the epistle of truth and love. The verse displays the greeting of love, closing the second epistle of John.
